authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual, Sector 56 and Subsector 561 Administrative and Support Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Census Bureau and Office of Management and Budget
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The NAICS manual is the official U.S. industry classification baseline. It defines administrative and support services as routine support activities for day-to-day operations and distinguishes support services from strategic management, public administration, and specialist industries.

authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual, 561110 Office Administrative Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Census Bureau and Office of Management and Budget
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NAICS 561110 is authoritative for office administrative services performed for others on a contract or fee basis. It anchors the lane to non-core client support workflows such as recordkeeping, personnel administration support, and logistics instead of client management.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Named administrative service and artifact identification

authoritative_sources.title: Administrative and Support Services: NAICS 561
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: BLS is the federal labor-statistics authority. Its NAICS 561 profile validates administrative and support services operating context, workforce terminology, and industry scope for scheduling and capacity planning assumptions.

authoritative_sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 5611: Office Administrative Services
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Census NAPCS product list grounds the product and service taxonomy for NAICS 5611, including contract or fee-basis office administrative services and non-core support processes used to distinguish the lane from generic scheduling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Administrative service product and evidence-context classification

authoritative_sources.title: O*NET 43-6014.00 Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Labor, Employment and Training Administration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: O*NET is the Department of Labor occupational information source. It provides public task terminology for scheduling appointments, maintaining calendars, arranging meetings, and administrative coordination.
used_for: artifact language for tenant terminology mapping

authoritative_sources.title: O*NET 43-5061.00 Production, Planning, and Expediting Clerks
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Labor, Employment and Training Administration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: O*NET defines work-order, staffing-table, schedule distribution, progress review, delay coordination, and documentation tasks that map directly to scheduling coordination workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Work-order, staffing-table, and schedule progress coordination

authoritative_sources.title: Schedule Assessment Guide: Best Practices for Project Schedules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GAO is the U.S. audit institution. Its schedule assessment guide is a recognized public reference for schedule logic, resources, constraints, updates, critical path, risk, and schedule documentation discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Schedule logic, update, constraint, and risk review
used_for: planning horizon and schedule update expectations

authoritative_sources.title: FAR Part 37 - Service Contracting
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FAR is the official federal acquisition regulation. Part 37 is a public reference for service requirements, performance-based services, surveillance planning, and service-delivery boundaries relevant to contracted administrative support services.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service requirement, SLA, and surveillance-boundary review

authoritative_sources.title: FAR Part 46 - Quality Assurance
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FAR quality assurance part provides public control vocabulary for inspection, acceptance, and quality assurance boundaries, supporting refusal of final service acceptance or quality signoff by the scheduling lane.

authoritative_sources.title: Fact Sheet #23: Overtime Pay Requirements of the FLSA
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Labor, Wage and Hour Division
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOL WHD is the federal wage-and-hour regulator. Its overtime guidance constrains staff scheduling outputs so the lane does not approve overtime, compensable time treatment, or wage-and-hour interpretations.

authoritative_sources.title: Fact Sheet #21: Recordkeeping Requirements under the FLSA
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Labor, Wage and Hour Division
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOL WHD recordkeeping guidance is authoritative for employer-maintained wage, hour, workweek, and schedule-related record expectations that constrain staffing-table and time-record evidence handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Staffing, time, and schedule evidence handling

authoritative_sources.title: Universal Electronic Records Management Requirements
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NARA is the U.S. federal records authority. Its ERM requirements provide public expectations for capture, metadata, access, retention, disposition, and auditability of administrative scheduling and handoff records.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Records capture, metadata, retention, and audit traceability

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, SP 800-53 Rev. 5
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is an authoritative public control catalog for access control, audit and accountability, privacy, identification, and system integrity expectations in scheduling, workflow, and evidence systems of record.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Access, privacy, audit, and system-control handling

workflow_stages.name: Administrative scheduling intake and service-boundary classification
workflow_stages.description: Capture request intent, client, SOW scope, service catalog category, queue, requested date, SLA, priority, requester, prior owner, and desired scheduling decision before treating the request as actionable.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite and source-record readiness validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate source queue, service ticket, client authorization, appointment or calendar record, staffing table, WFM extract, privacy class, evidence bundle, and authoritative system mapping before schedule recommendations or handoffs.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess backlog, queue aging, staff or seat capacity, skills and queue eligibility, client service windows, blackout periods, SLA thresholds, labor-hour caveats, cross-client segregation, and source-record conflicts.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Schedule, sequence, and allocation recommendation support
workflow_stages.description: Produce recommendation-only sequencing, capacity, deferment, and schedule options using approved priority rules, planning horizons, service-level targets, documented constraints, and evidence links.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Handoff ownership, exception, and approval escalation
workflow_stages.description: Identify prior owner, next owner, required artifact set, blocker state, approval-needed tradeoffs, and adjacent specialist routing when work cannot safely proceed or authority shifts.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Refresh, audit, and closure evidence capture
workflow_stages.description: Record source refresh posture, tenant assumptions, schedule recommendation traceability, handoff outcome, unresolved blockers, and refusal or orchestrator-return notes without claiming final approval.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: client master record, contract, SOW, order form, service catalog, RACI, approval matrix, escalation matrix, and scheduling SOP
ticket, case, BPM work item, queue record, backlog extract, appointment request, service calendar, queue aging sheet, and exception queue
WFM extract, staffing table, roster, seat pool, skills or queue eligibility matrix, shift window, time-off record, and capacity forecast
client service window, blackout period, SLA target, priority matrix, tie-break rule, planning horizon, and service-level report
intake packet, authorization record, source document link, correspondence, evidence bundle, privacy classification, retention class, and handoff checklist
schedule recommendation, allocation option, deferred-work set, constraint log, approval-needed summary, handoff status summary, and blocked-dependency escalation note
audit log, record metadata, access approval, cross-client segregation note, source conflict summary, GRC issue, and remediation or exception tracker
systems_of_record: client relationship management or client master system
contract lifecycle management and SOW repository
ticketing, workflow, BPM, case management, or queue management system
scheduling, appointment, calendar, or booking system
workforce management, timekeeping, capacity planning, or staffing system
service-level reporting or business intelligence system
document management or electronic records management system
GRC, policy, exception, or control management system
audit evidence repository and approved correspondence archive

allowed: Classify scheduling coordination requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Assess whether authoritative records support a bounded timing, sequencing, staffing, capacity, or allocation recommendation.
Recommend schedule or allocation options, deferrals, capacity-risk posture, handoff routing, and blocker escalation with evidence references.
Determine the correct next owner and required artifact set for handoff when the service catalog, RACI, and queue owner map are sufficient.
escalate: Required service catalog, SOW, queue, staffing, calendar, WFM, privacy, source-record, tie-break, or owner-map evidence is missing or materially conflicting.
Demand exceeds approved capacity, service levels, or staffing envelopes and the tradeoff requires approval outside the lane.
Priority rules, client commitments, cross-client staffing restrictions, or system-of-record states conflict materially.

Ownership is ambiguous, duplicated, stale, or split across administrative operations, shared services, contact-center operations, workforce, records, compliance, HR, payroll, finance, legal, or client management.
refuse: Approve final staffing, overtime, budget, service-level degradation, client-facing commitments, contract changes, payroll corrections, or HR decisions.
Interpret wage-and-hour, accommodation, privacy, legal, compliance, or contract rules as final authority.
Proceed with a schedule commitment or handoff when required source records, approvals, privacy classification, or handoff artifacts are missing.
Treat administrative support and business services as a license to absorb travel, event, HR, payroll, legal, records governance, contact-center execution, or client management work.

domain_failure_modes: committing a client schedule from a stale queue extract or non-authoritative calendar
sequencing administrative service work without validating SOW scope, service catalog category, SLA target, and owner map
using generic capacity language while ignoring seat pools, staff eligibility, client windows, cross-client segregation, or evidence readiness
inventing priority rules when multiple client queues compete for the same trained staff and no approved tie-break exists
approving overtime, staffing, service-level degradation, or client commitments under the guise of scheduling
handing work to the next queue without prior-owner note, source evidence, due date, blocker state, or required artifact set
collapsing NAICS 561 administrative support into unrelated travel, event, payroll, HR, legal, compliance, quality, or client management authority
synthesizing conflicting public, tenant, SOW, labor, privacy, or queue-playbook rules into a confident recommendation instead of escalation

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Demand exceeds capacity or approved service levels
Conflicting priorities with no approved tie-break rule
Stakeholder asks for final approval the lane cannot grant
Ownership is ambiguous across multiple teams
A handoff is attempted without the required context
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant SOWs, service catalogs, staffing rules, client blackout periods, and queue owner maps can materially change feasible administrative scheduling options.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This is resolved operationally by requiring tenant-specific service model, capacity envelope, priority matrix, and source-of-truth mappings before deterministic recommendations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Wage-and-hour, accommodation, privacy, contract, and service-level requirements vary by jurisdiction, worker type, client contract, and tenant policy.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack treats these as authority boundaries. The lane may surface schedule impact and missing facts, but final interpretation and approval route to HR, payroll, legal, privacy, finance, compliance, or client/service owners.

notes: The research baseline intentionally covers workflow, evidence, terminology, authority boundaries, and failure handling instead of generic calendar management only.

The lane remains recommendation-only even when it produces detailed schedule, capacity, allocation, or handoff guidance; service owners, workforce, HR, payroll, finance, legal, compliance, privacy, contract, and client management owners retain final authority.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated use because all known variation is handled through mandatory runtime retrieval, explicit refusal, and orchestrator-return requirements.
